items.rationale: He views decentralization as the key that unlocks web3, reduces consumer risk, and—critically—moves tokens outside the securities regime, giving all projects a legal path forward.
items.age_note: Consistent theme from 2023 through June 2024; intensity rising after May 2024 ETH ETF approvals reinforced the legal thesis.

citations.quote: The country that properly incentivizes decentralization is going to dominate blockchain innovation and will reap the benefits.
decentralization moves tokens outside of the US securities law regime
We're entering a new era of decentralization.

items.rationale: The DUNA solves legal existence, taxes, limited liability, and privacy for DAOs while preserving decentralization—he calls it years-in-the-making and a major unlock for builders.
items.age_note: Peak excitement March 2024 at DUNA enactment; live July 1, 2024.

citations.quote: Wyoming just enacted a game-changing law for web3
This is a major breakthrough.

items.rationale: He insists tokens aren't equity and the design space is far larger; favors B2B/credible-neutrality models and value accrual over user-acquisition speculation.
items.age_note: Core stance from 2023-04, restated and refined through 2024-04 token-launch playbook—persisted and reinforced.

citations.quote: Tokens aren't equity. The design space is so much bigger.
Application token models should focus more on B2B and less on B2C.

items.rationale: He argues disintermediation obviates intermediary regulation, that DEX frontends aren't brokers, and that SEC attacks on DEXs should ultimately fail.

citations.quote: Most DEX frontends aren't brokers.
how that strategy could be used to target decentralized exchanges (DEXs) next and why those attacks should fail

items.rationale: Sees app/client-layer decentralization as critical for richer services like social media, driving governance minimization and credible neutrality.

citations.quote: this type of decentralization is going to become critical for more robust web3 digital services like social
Excited to see more of these models start to emerge.

items.rationale: Frames privacy as a fundamental right with legitimate use cases outweighing illicit ones; bullish on zk protocols built responsibly to mitigate regulator concerns.

citations.quote: using zk tech to build privacy preserving protocols that will be a net good for society
There are more legitimate use cases for privacy than illegitimate use cases.

items.rationale: Considers raising security standards among the most critical needs in crypto; actively involved with SEAL org.

citations.quote: Few things in crypto are more critical than upleveling security standards and practices.

items.rationale: Calls it the future and urges the US to keep the technology onshore rather than push it abroad.
items.age_note: Single recent statement (2024-04), not yet restated—persisted.

citations.quote: Jolt is an incredible step forward for decentralized computing. This technology is the future

items.rationale: He believes Gensler's SEC aims to ban the technology, fosters deliberate uncertainty that emboldens bad actors and harms good builders, and has refused real guidance for five years.
items.age_note: Dominant, escalating concern across the whole corpus; late-June 2024 Chevron overturn gives partial relief on rulemaking overreach but enforcement pace unchanged.

citations.quote: Today is the five year anniversary of the last time the SEC provided constructive guidance on crypto.
the SEC's endgame for crypto/web3 in the United States is about one thing: banning the technology.
The answer is politics.

items.rationale: Sees the 2024 memecoin wave as purely speculative grift with no underlying value, damaging the industry's regulatory standing and distracting from productive use cases.
items.age_note: Sharpened markedly in April 2024 as memecoin mania peaked.

citations.quote: Memecoins are the crypto equivalent of chain emails.
Championing crypto casino culture as an expression of individual freedom isn't a principled view
The market likes to gamble. That doesn't mean that gambling is productive.

items.rationale: Fixed investor token allocations misalign incentives and transfer value from founders; short lockups enable VCs to dump on retail via pump-and-dumps.
items.age_note: Repeated Dec 2023–Feb 2024; an active, current campaign.

citations.quote: Fixed token allocations are predatory.
Short lockups lead to pump and dumps. That's bad for the industry.
4 year/1 year cliff token lockups should be standard.

items.rationale: Concerned policymakers exploit illicit-finance narratives to erode financial privacy, ignoring the Third-Party Doctrine and pushing toward surveillance-state CBDCs.

citations.quote: Elizabeth Warren's Patriot Act Part 2.
The problem with people like Warren is that their answer to everything is 'more surveillance'.

items.rationale: Warns that concentrating power in a single stablecoin issuer (or a CBDC) undermines decentralization across web3 and creates regulatory capture.

citations.quote: Concentrated power in the hands of any single stablecoin issuer undermines decentralization across web3.
Agree this inches closer to a potential surveillance state CBDC.

items.rationale: Acknowledges high-FDV/low-float launches as a significant structural problem, though he sees longer gradual lockups (not short ones) as the remedy.

citations.quote: Agree that high FDV/low float is a significant problem

items.rationale: He reads the ETH futures approval and 2024 spot ETF approvals as the SEC effectively conceding ETH is not a security, enshrining decentralization as a path out of securities law for the whole industry. (A regulatory-status view, not a price call.)
items.age_note: View built Jan 2024 (futures) and confirmed/strengthened May 2024 (spot ETF approvals).

citations.quote: These approvals enshrine the SEC's position in 2018 - ETH is not a security.
The SEC capitulated on the question of whether ETH is a security in October when it approved the ETH futures products.

items.rationale: Considers USDC likely okay under securities law because Circle KYCs at issuance, doesn't rehypothecate, and isn't the issuer offering an Anchor-style yield scheme.

citations.quote: USDC is likely okay, but yield bearing stablecoins should take note.

items.rationale: Warns that any stablecoin packaged with promoter-driven yield can be offered as a security; CeFi retail-yield products on stablecoins should be careful.

citations.quote: yield bearing stablecoins should take note. Any commodity (including a stablecoin) can be offered in a securities offering.

items.rationale: Uses Terra as the canonical example of a non-decentralized issuer/operator running an unsustainable yield scheme that was rightly a securities offering—the failure mode others should avoid.
items.age_note: Retrospective commentary after the 2024 SEC win; Terra already collapsed, cited as a lesson rather than a live position.

citations.quote: operating the Anchor protocol and its bullshit 20% yield, which also wasn't decentralized

risk_regime.rationale: Jennings is structurally bullish on decentralized technology as the future internet infrastructure, and his most recent (May–June 2024) statements lean constructive: ETH ETF approvals validating the decentralization-as-legal-path thesis, FIT21 advancing, the DUNA going live, and the Chevron overturn constraining agency overreach. That optimism is heavily conditioned, however, by acute concern over a hostile US SEC and a memecoin/casino speculative cycle he sees as self-harming—so his constructiveness is about the technology's long-term trajectory and regulatory progress, not broad near-term asset risk-taking.

notes: Jennings is a16z crypto's GC/Head of Decentralization, so his 'market view' is expressed through legal, policy, and token-design framing rather than price or allocation calls; conviction scores reflect rhetorical intensity, not trading sizing. His single most load-bearing belief—threaded through nearly every entry—is that decentralization both creates web3's value and is the determinant of correct regulatory treatment (the 'Regulate Web3 Apps, Not Protocols' / Howey-decentralization thesis). Recency weighting matters in two places: (1) his ETH stance evolved from cautious legal hedging (2023) to firm 'ETH is not a security' after the 2024 approvals—reported as current; (2) his tone shifted from broadly defensive/embattled (mid-2023 SEC enforcement wave) to cautiously winning (mid-2024 court wins, FIT21, DUNA, Chevron). No genuine reversals were found; older stances (tokens-aren't-equity, fat-protocol-thesis-is-wrong, app-not-protocol regulation) persisted and were reinforced. He is notably NOT a memecoin/speculation bull and repeatedly distances himself from 'code-is-law' / ignore-the-law absolutism, favoring engagement and lobbying over building purely 'anti-fragile' systems.
